narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 93% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for law school admission was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key factors from your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. Most notably, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 pulled heavily toward the failure prediction, as it placed you well below the typical student's mid-range performance in that area. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2 had a significant pushing effect in the same direction, signaling to the model lower early academic standing compared to most applicants who rank around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 28 also contributed substantially toward the failure outlook, falling considerably short of the average around 37 that the model associates with stronger bar exam success. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing since it's above the typical 3.21 average. Your middle family income bracket had a modest influence pushing toward failure, while your full-time enrollment plan offered a tiny positive boost, aligning with the vast majority of students. Overall, compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows lower decile rankings and LSAT that outweighed your GPA strength, tipping the scales against admission. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—you have clear strengths like that GPA that show promise. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 37 average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources or academic advising to build a profile that reassures models like this one. Many students in similar situations improve and succeed on retries, so keep pushing—you've got this. We're here to support your next steps.
